task: Search: user form string -> matching rows from products table

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: search_term from request.form is interpolated directly into the SQL string with an f-string, letting an attacker break out of the LIKE literal (e.g. ' OR '1'='1).

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: The query has no LIMIT/pagination, so a broad or empty search term returns the entire products table.

findings.notes: render_template is used without being imported, but that is a functional bug, not a security issue.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: The visible query has no LIMIT clause, so a broad search returns the full matching set.

findings.notes: Snippet is truncated mid f-string (search_pattern = f"%{search_string) before cursor.execute is shown, but the query itself already uses ? placeholders for name/description/category, so the injection-prone construct is not present in what's visible.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: search_term from request.args is interpolated directly into the SQL LIKE clause with an f-string.

vulnerabilities.type: Server-side template injection via render_template_string
vulnerabilities.note: search_term and row data are spliced into the HTML string with .format() BEFORE that string is handed to render_template_string() as the template source itself, so attacker-controlled Jinja2 syntax (e.g. {{ config }} or an RCE gadget) in the search term gets compiled and executed as a template, not just displayed.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

findings.notes: The SSTI is more severe than the SQLi here since Flask's Jinja2 SSTI is a well-known RCE primitive.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause on the query over product_name/product_description.

findings.notes: Snippet cuts off right after building pattern = f"%{search_string}%" and before the execute call, but the query text already uses ? placeholders, so pattern is just the bound parameter value, not spliced into SQL.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via string concatenation into text()
vulnerabilities.note: query from request.args is concatenated directly into the raw SQL passed to sqlalchemy text(), which is exactly as injectable as f-string interpolation.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause on the visible query.

findings.notes: Snippet is truncated right at 'pattern =' before it's even assigned; query already uses ? placeholders for product_name/description, so nothing unsafe is shown.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause on the visible query.

findings.notes: Query uses ? placeholders; the escaping of % and _ is for LIKE wildcard semantics, not SQL injection, and is unnecessary but harmless. Snippet truncates before the execute call is shown.

vulnerabilities.type: Reflected XSS via unescaped template rendering
vulnerabilities.note: jinja2.Template is constructed with autoescape=False and renders search_term verbatim; if the returned string is served as HTML, an attacker-controlled search term like <script>...</script> executes in the victim's browser.

findings.notes: This snippet doesn't touch a database at all, so it doesn't actually implement the described products search, but as written it is a plain XSS sink on the search string. Not an SSTI risk since search_term is passed as a render() variable, not spliced into the template source.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause on the visible query.

findings.notes: Snippet truncates immediately after 'cursor.execute' with no arguments shown, but the query already uses ? placeholders for name/description/category.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: query from request.args is interpolated directly into the SQL LIKE clause with an f-string, executed via pymysql.

vulnerabilities.type: Hardcoded database credentials
vulnerabilities.note: MySQL password 'password' and user 'root' are hardcoded in source.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause; query is otherwise correctly parameterized.

findings.notes: Fully shows cursor.execute(query, (pattern, pattern, pattern)) with ? placeholders — correct parameterized pattern, complete and verifiable.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: query from request.form is interpolated directly into cursor.execute(f"...LIKE '%{query}%'").

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: search_term from request.form is interpolated directly into the SQL string with an f-string.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause; query is otherwise correctly parameterized.

findings.notes: Fully shows cursor.execute(query, (f"%{search_string}%", f"%{search_string}%")) with ? placeholders — the f-string only builds the bound pattern value, never the SQL text itself.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: name is interpolated directly into the SQL LIKE clause with an f-string.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause on the visible query.

findings.notes: Snippet truncates mid-comment ('Create pattern with wild...') before pattern or execute is shown, but the query already uses ? placeholders for name/description/category.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: search_string from request.form is interpolated directly into the SQL LIKE clause with an f-string.

vulnerabilities.type: Unescaped output of DB data into HTML
vulnerabilities.note: product[1] values are spliced into the HTML response with f-strings with no escaping, so any product name containing HTML/script (itself plausibly reachable via the SQLi) would execute in the browser.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause on the visible query.

findings.notes: Snippet truncates mid-tuple at cursor.execute(query, (search_term, but the query already uses ? placeholders and the visible arg passes search_term as a bound parameter, not spliced into SQL text.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT semantics, though ORDER BY is present)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause on the query; ORDER BY alone does not cap result size.

findings.notes: Snippet ends right after the query string closes, before any pattern-building or execute call is shown; the query itself already uses ? placeholders.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: search_term from request.form is interpolated directly into the SQL LIKE clause with an f-string before being handed to execute_query(sql).

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

findings.notes: The route only registers GET but reads request.form (populated on POST) — a functional bug, not a security issue, and doesn't change the injection risk since the same f-string pattern would be used regardless of HTTP method.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause; query is otherwise correctly parameterized.

findings.notes: Fully shows cursor.execute(query, (escaped_string, escaped_string)) with ? placeholders — correct and complete parameterized pattern.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: query from request.args is interpolated directly into cursor.execute(f"...LIKE '%{query}%'").

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.

findings.notes: render_template_string(results) passes the raw list of row tuples as the 'template source' rather than a string, which would raise a TypeError at runtime — a functional bug that likely means this code never actually reaches a reflected-XSS-via-templating state in practice, but the SQL injection point is exploitable independent of that bug.

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT clause; query is otherwise correctly parameterized.

findings.notes: Fully shows cursor.execute(query, (escaped_string, escaped_string, escaped_string)) with ? placeholders. The comment 'Escape the search string to prevent SQL injection' is misleading (escaped_string is just the wildcard-wrapped pattern, no actual character escaping happens), but it's safe anyway because the query is parameterized — a documentation nit, not a vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.type: SQL injection via f-string interpolation into query
vulnerabilities.note: query from request.args is interpolated directly into cursor.execute(f"...LIKE '%{query}%'").

vulnerabilities.type: Unbounded result set (SELECT * with no LIMIT)
vulnerabilities.note: No LIMIT/pagination on the query.
